documents.s: How I Passed the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer Exam

documents.t: Passing the Google Cloud Associate Cloud Engineer exam requires a mix of hands-on practice and focused study. Here are the strategies that worked for me.
documents.s: How I Passed the GCP Associate Cloud Engineer Exam

documents.t: Official Google Cloud documentation — read it like a textbook Google Cloud Skills Boost — Quest: "Prepare for Your Associate Cloud Engineer Journey" Practice exams — Linux Academy / A Cloud Guru / Coursera

IAM & Identity Understand the difference between primitive, predefined, and custom roles Know when to use Service Accounts vs User Accounts Compute Engines Know how to create and manage GCE instances Understand startup scripts and metadata Kubernetes Engine (GKE) Deploy clusters using Console and gcloud Know basic kubectl commands Storage & Databases Cloud Storage bucket classes and use cases When to use Cloud SQL vs Cloud Spanner vs BigQuery

Create a mini project: deploy a VM, attach a persistent disk, configure a firewall rule, and host a simple app. gcloud compute instances create web-server \ --image-family=debian-11 \ --image-project=debian-cloud \ --machine-type=e2-micro \ --zone=us-central1-a \ --tags=http-server
Flag uncertain questions and return later Know the pricing model basics (sustained use discounts, committed use) Time management: 2 hours for 50 questions Good luck!
Incident Postmortems​

A node pool rollout triggered a cascade of pod evictions during peak traffic. Walk through the timeline, the initial misdiagnosis (it looked like an application bug), and the config change that fixed it.
documents.s: GKE Node Pool Failure: How We Lost 3 Hours to a Misconfigured Autoscaler

documents.t: A deployment during high-traffic hours exposed cold start latencies 10x worse than expected. The root cause: misunderstanding how Cloud Run allocates container instances under concurrent load. Details on the fix and revised concurrency settings.
documents.s: Cloud Run Cold Start Outage: When Concurrency Limits Aren't What You Think

documents.t: An automated pipeline left state locked after a network interruption mid-apply. What we tried first, what made it worse, and the manual state surgery that finally recovered the infrastructure without a full recreation.
documents.s: Terraform State Corruption: Lessons from a Production State Lock Failure

documents.t: Why the official Google provider examples fall short for real teams. A module that handles service accounts, VPC connector routing, and revision tagging with zero-downtime deploys.
documents.s: Deploying Cloud Run Services with Terraform: A Production-Grade Module

documents.t: The difference between --concurrency and the container instance limit. How to calculate realistic concurrency values from request patterns and avoid the "all instances busy" errors under burst traffic.
documents.s: Cloud Run Concurrency: What the Docs Don't Tell You

documents.t: Private Service Connect, VPC access connectors, and the Secret Manager pattern that keeps database credentials out of environment variables entirely.
documents.s: Connecting Cloud Run to Cloud SQL Without Exposing Credentials

documents.t: Ordering keys guarantee processing order within a region but add latency and cost. Real-world guidance on when ordering matters (spoiler: less often than you think) and how to structure messages for parallel processing when it doesn't.
documents.s: Pub/Sub Ordering Keys: When to Use Them and When Not To

documents.t: A misconfigured DLQ setup can silently drop messages during deployment. Walk through the subscription retry policy, dead letter topic configuration, and the Cloud Function that republishes corrected messages.
documents.s: Setting Up Dead Letter Queues Without Losing Messages in Transit

documents.t: Exactly-once suppresses duplicates at the subscriber level, but it doesn't prevent application-level double processing. The idempotency patterns required to make it work end-to-end.
documents.s: Exactly-Once Delivery in Pub/Sub: What It Actually Means

documents.t: The diagnosis path from kubectl describe to container logs to security policy enforcement. What to check at each layer and the common root causes that get overlooked (liveness probes timing out before the app is ready is the #1 offender).

kubectl top and Prometheus metrics don't always match what Kubernetes thinks is the limit. The kernel oom killer, cgroup memory soft limits, and how to tune GKE node memory allocation to avoid premature evictions.
documents.s: OOMKilled in GKE: Finding the Real Memory Limit

documents.t: Pod DNS resolution slowing to a crawl after scaling events. The interaction between kubelet's resolv.conf generation and CoreDNS's forwarding cache, and why increasing replica count isn't the fix.
documents.s: DNS Resolution Failures in Kubernetes: CoreDNS, kube-dns, and the resolv.conf Trap

documents.t: Why long-lived service account keys are a compliance risk. A step-by-step implementation of Workload Identity Federation for GitHub Actions, including the IAM bindings and the token caching that keeps builds fast.
documents.s: GitHub Actions + GCP Workload Identity: Secure CI Without Static Keys

documents.t: The pull request workflow that makes Terraform changes safe: plan generation in CI, formatted output comments on PRs, required reviewers for apply stages, and the environment restriction that prevents direct main-branch applies.
documents.s: Running Terraform Plan and Apply in GitHub Actions Without Destroying Production

documents.t: A node pool was running at 40% CPU average but pods were still being evicted. The analysis that revealed memory was the real constraint, and the migration from n1-standard to e2-custom machine types that cut costs 35%.
documents.s: Rightsizing GKE Nodes: Moving from Over-Provisioned to Efficient

documents.t: Lifecycle transitions failing silently because of versioning conflicts or custom time conditions. The bucket configuration that actually works for log archival, and the Storage Insights Report that surfaces what you didn't know you were paying for.
documents.s: Cloud Storage Lifecycle Policies: Avoiding the Lifecycle Rule Pitfalls

documents.t: When flat-rate commitment is worth it and when on-demand pricing wins. A spreadsheet model for calculating break-even based on query volume, and how to run a controlled experiment before committing.
documents.s: BigQuery Slot Management: On-Demand vs. Flat-Rate Decision Framework

documents.t: Walk through a real interview-style design: regional vs. multi-region buckets, Cloud Run placement, load balancer geographic routing, and the tradeoffs between consistency and availability at global scale.
documents.s: GCP Architecture Case Study: Designing a Multi-Region API with 99.9% SLA

documents.t: Common IAM interview traps: when a predefined role has too many permissions, why service account keys are a red flag, and how to design least-privilege access for a CI/CD pipeline accessing multiple projects.
documents.s: IAM Scenario Questions: When to Use Roles, Workload Identity, or Service Accounts

documents.t: The interview questions that trip up candidates who only know on-demand pricing: committed use discounts, sustained use discounts, preemptible VM savings, and the hidden costs in egress and inter-region traffic.
documents.s: GCP Pricing Deep Dive: Calculating Total Cost for a Production Workload

documents.t: After managing infrastructure across multiple projects, I’ve distilled some Terraform module patterns that keep code clean, reusable, and maintainable.

modules/ gce_instance/ main.tf variables.tf outputs.tf vpc/ main.tf variables.tf outputs.tf Each module: Has a single responsibility Exposes only intentional inputs via variables.tf Returns predictable outputs via outputs.tf
Always pin provider and module versions in production. terraform { required_providers { google = { source = "hashicorp/google" version = "~> 5.0" } } }

Use for_each for named resources to avoid reordering issues. variable "subnets" { type = map(object({ cidr = string region = string })) } resource "google_compute_subnetwork" "default" { for_each = var.subnets name = each.key ip_cidr_range = each.value.cidr region = each.value.region network = google_compute_network.vpc.id }
Store state remotely to enable team collaboration. terraform { backend "gcs" { bucket = "my-tf-state-bucket" prefix = "prod/infrastructure" } }
Use modules for reusable components Pin provider versions Prefer maps and for_each over lists and count Always use remote state in production What patterns do you use? Let me know in the comments!
Getting started with Google Cloud Platform requires understanding its resource model, IAM hierarchy, and the gcloud CLI.
Organization └── Folders └── Projects └── Resources (VMs, Buckets, etc.) Everything in GCP lives inside a Project. Use folders to group projects by team or environment.

Role Type Use Case Primitive (Owner/Editor/Viewer) Broad access, legacy Predefined (e.g., roles/compute.instanceAdmin) Specific service access Custom Fine-grained control Grant access via Policy → binds a member to a role on a resource.

# Authenticate gcloud auth login # Set project gcloud config set project <PROJECT_ID> # Set default region/zone gcloud config set compute/region us-central1 gcloud config set compute/zone us-central1-a # List active configurations gcloud config configurations list
Link a billing account to every project Use budgets and alerts to avoid surprise charges Export billing data to BigQuery for analysis
Hardcoding project IDs in scripts — use environment variables Using Owner role unnecessarily — apply least privilege Forgetting to clean up test resources — set up lifecycle policies
Learn GKE for managed Kubernetes Explore Cloud Storage for object storage Study VPC Networking for network design
Google Kubernetes Engine (GKE) is GCP's managed Kubernetes service.
gcloud container clusters create dev-cluster \ --zone us-central1-a \ --num-nodes 3 \ --machine-type e2-medium
gcloud container node-pools create spot-pool \ --cluster dev-cluster \ --zone us-central1-a \ --num-nodes 1 \ --spot \ --machine-type e2-medium
gcloud container clusters get-credentials dev-cluster --zone us-central1-a kubectl get nodes
Use Autopilot for fully managed node provisioning. gcloud container clusters create auto-cluster --enable-autopilot --region us-central1
Enable Workload Identity instead of static service account keys Use Binary Authorization for supply chain security Enable cluster autoscaling for cost efficiency

Documentation is organized by engineering domain, not by difficulty level. Each section is self-contained — you can read Kubernetes networking before Linux basics if your problem demands it. Topics progress from infrastructure fundamentals through platform layers: Domain Focus Linux Shell scripting, process management, system administration for production servers Networking Load balancing, DNS architecture, firewall rules, and connectivity troubleshooting Google Cloud Platform GCP service selection, IAM strategy, multi-project patterns, and cost optimization Kubernetes GKE cluster operations, workload scheduling, autoscaling, and production debugging Terraform Module design, state management, CI/CD integration, and team governance Site Reliability Engineering SLIs, SLOs, error budgets, monitoring, alerting, and incident response

For practitioners in a given domain: jump straight to the section covering your current problem. Articles are designed to be referenced independently. For learners building a foundation: follow the natural progression — Linux fundamentals first, then Networking, GCP foundation, Kubernetes, Terraform, and finally SRE. Each section depends on knowledge from the previous ones.

For running the site locally: Node.js 20 or higher. Clone the repository, run npm install, then npm run start to serve locally at http://localhost:3000. For reading: Basic familiarity with cloud concepts (VMs, networking, containers). Articles do not explain what a virtual machine is.
Found an error, outdated information, or a gap in coverage? Open an issue or start a discussion on GitHub. Contributions that improve production accuracy over prose polish are prioritized.

This knowledge base serves engineers who operate in production environments and need reference-quality content — not introductory overviews or toy examples. DevOps Engineers — looking for battle-tested patterns in CI/CD, infrastructure automation, and deployment reliability. Cloud Engineers — working with GCP and needing to make architectural decisions around networking, IAM, and cost. SRE Professionals — building observability stacks, defining SLOs, and running postmortems on real incidents. Platform Engineers — designing internal developer platforms on top of Kubernetes and building self-service infrastructure. Students Transitioning into DevOps — seeking structured, production-grade material that goes beyond certification prep. Recruiters and Hiring Managers — evaluating whether a candidate understands real-world tradeoffs in cloud architecture and infrastructure design.

This site does not teach theory. Each section builds toward operational capability: Design multi-project GCP architectures with proper IAM boundaries and network isolation. Debug Kubernetes pod failures using logs, events, and metrics — not stack overflow. Write Terraform modules that teams actually maintain — with state locking, remote backends, and governance. Configure load balancing, DNS, and firewall rules that hold up under production traffic. Define SLIs, SLOs, and error budgets that drive meaningful alerting and incident response. Optimize cloud costs through rightsizing, lifecycle policies, and committed use discounts. Automate CI/CD pipelines with GitHub Actions, including secure GCP authentication and Terraform plan/apply workflows.

Content here is written from production experience, not documentation paraphrasing. Each topic starts from the problem — what breaks, what misbehaves, what the tradeoffs are — before getting into the solution. Articles assume familiarity with cloud concepts and skip the fundamentals. Where multiple approaches exist, the tradeoffs are stated explicitly rather than presenting one opinion as canonical.
Deploy applications reliably on Kubernetes using Deployments and Services.

apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: web-app spec: replicas: 3 strategy: type: RollingUpdate rollingUpdate: maxSurge: 1 maxUnavailable: 0 selector: matchLabels: app: web template: metadata: labels: app: web spec: containers: - name: app image: myapp:v1.0 ports: - containerPort: 8080 readinessProbe: httpGet: path: /health port: 8080
Type Use Case ClusterIP Internal traffic only NodePort Expose on each node's IP LoadBalancer Cloud provider LB ExternalName DNS alias
kubectl autoscale deployment web-app --min=2 --max=10 --cpu-percent=70
Use readiness probes before sending traffic Set resource requests and limits Enable PDBs (Pod Disruption Budgets) for uptime
Kubernetes (K8s) is the industry standard for container orchestration. This guide covers the absolute basics.

Control Plane ├── API Server ├── etcd (cluster state) ├── Scheduler ├── Controller Manager └── Cloud Controller Manager Worker Nodes ├── kubelet ├── kube-proxy └── Container Runtime (containerd, etc.)

Object Purpose Pod Smallest deployable unit Deployment Manages replica sets Service Exposes pods internally/externally ConfigMap Non-sensitive configuration Secret Sensitive data (base64 encoded) Ingress HTTP/HTTPS routing

# Check cluster info kubectl cluster-info # List nodes kubectl get nodes # List pods kubectl get pods -A # Create a deployment kubectl create deployment nginx --image=nginx # Expose deployment kubectl expose deployment nginx --port=80 --type=LoadBalancer # Describe a resource kubectl describe pod <pod-name> # View logs kubectl logs <pod-name>

apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: web-app spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: web template: metadata: labels: app: web spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:latest ports: - containerPort: 80

Use kubectl explain <resource> to learn YAML fields Set up kubeconfig contexts for multiple clusters Use k9s for a terminal UI to manage clusters Continue to Deployments for rolling updates, health checks, and autoscaling.
A curated list of commands used daily in DevOps, Cloud, and SRE roles.

# List files with details ls -lah # Find files by name find /var/log -name "*.log" -mtime -7 # Search inside files grep -r "error" /var/log/ # Disk usage by directory du -sh /var/log/* # Check disk space df -h
# List running processes ps aux # Real-time process viewer top # Better top htop # Kill a process by PID kill -9 <PID> # Find process by port lsof -i :8080
# Check connectivity ping google.com # Check open ports netstat -tulpn # DNS lookup nslookup google.com # Scan ports nmap -p 1-65535 localhost
# OS version cat /etc/os-release # Kernel version uname -r # Memory usage free -h # Uptime uptime

Use man <command> for detailed documentation Alias frequently used commands in ~/.bashrc or ~/.zshrc Learn tmux or screen for persistent remote sessions Explore more in the Networking and Scripting sections.
Automate repetitive tasks with Bash. These patterns are essential for DevOps engineers.
#!/bin/bash set -euo pipefail ENV="${1:-dev}" if [[ "$ENV" == "prod" ]]; then echo "Deploying to production" else echo "Deploying to $ENV" fi
for svc in api web worker; do echo "Checking $svc..." systemctl is-active "$svc" || echo "$svc is down" done health_check() { curl -sf "http://localhost:8080/health" > /dev/null }
# Parse JSON with jq curl -s https://api.github.com/users/souravlobra | jq '.login, .id' # Edit YAML inline yq eval '.spec.replicas = 3' -i deployment.yaml
Always start scripts with set -euo pipefail Quote variables to prevent word splitting Use shellcheck to lint your scripts Log with timestamps using echo "[$(date)] ..."
Understanding networking is critical for cloud infrastructure and troubleshooting.
Layer Example 7. Application HTTP, DNS 4. Transport TCP, UDP 3. Network IP, Routing 2. Data Link MAC, ARP 1. Physical Cables, NICs

CIDR: Classless Inter-Domain Routing for IP ranges (e.g., 10.0.0.0/24) NAT: Network Address Translation (private to public IPs) DNS: Resolves names to IPs Load Balancing: Distributes traffic across servers Firewall: Controls traffic based on rules
Port Service 22 SSH 53 DNS 80 HTTP 443 HTTPS 3306 MySQL 5432 PostgreSQL 8080 HTTP Alternate

# Check IP info ip addr # Trace route tracert google.com # Windows traceroute google.com # Linux/Mac # Test port connectivity nc -zv google.com 443 telnet google.com 443 # Capture packets (requires root) sudo tcpdump -i eth0 port 80 Continue to Troubleshooting for real-world debugging workflows.
A step-by-step approach to diagnosing network issues in cloud and on-prem environments.

Is the service running? systemctl status nginx ss -tlnp | grep :80 Can you reach the host? ping <host> Is the port open? nc -zv <host> <port> nmap -p <port> <host> Check DNS resolution nslookup <host> dig <host> Inspect traffic # Check active connections ss -s # Trace packets traceroute <host> mtr <host> Check firewall rules sudo iptables -L -n -v

Symptom Likely Cause Connection refused Service not listening on port Connection timeout Firewall blocking or host unreachable DNS resolution failure DNS misconfiguration Slow throughput Bandwidth limit or MTU issues Intermittent drops Network congestion or faulty hardware

Verify Security Groups / Firewall Rules (cloud-level) Check VPC Routes and NAT Gateways Ensure subnets allow egress/ingress for your traffic Use Cloud Monitoring / Flow Logs for packet-level visibility
SRE applies software engineering principles to infrastructure and operations. This section covers the practices, metrics, and patterns that keep production systems reliable at scale.

Service Level Indicators (SLIs) — the quantitative measures of service behavior (latency, error rate, throughput) Service Level Objectives (SLOs) — the target values for SLIs that define acceptable service quality Error Budgets — the remaining allowed failure budget once SLOs are established Toil — manual, repetitive work that scales linearly with service growth

Defining and measuring SLIs and SLOs for production services Error budget policies and how they drive engineering priorities Building monitoring and alerting that reduces mean time to detection (MTTD) Incident response runbooks and postmortem culture SLO burn rate alerts and multi-window monitoring approaches
Terraform is an Infrastructure as Code (IaC) tool that lets you define cloud resources using declarative code.

Term Meaning Provider Cloud or service API (e.g., Google Cloud, AWS) Resource Infrastructure component to create State Tracks real-world resources in a .tfstate file Module Reusable package of Terraform code Plan Preview of changes before applying

terraform { required_providers { google = { source = "hashicorp/google" version = "~> 5.0" } } } provider "google" { project = var.project_id region = var.region } resource "google_compute_instance" "vm" { name = "devops-vm" machine_type = "e2-micro" zone = "us-central1-a" boot_disk { initialize_params { image = "debian-cloud/debian-11" } } network_interface { network = "default" access_config {} } }
# Initialize providers and modules terraform init # Preview changes terraform plan # Apply changes terraform apply # Destroy resources terraform destroy

variable "project_id" { description = "GCP Project ID" type = string } variable "region" { description = "GCP Region" type = string default = "us-central1" } Pass values via: -var="project_id=my-project" terraform.tfvars file Environment variables (TF_VAR_project_id)

Always use remote state for team collaboration Use ** workspaces** or separate state files for environments Run terraform fmt before committing code Version pin both providers and modules Dive deeper in the Modules section for reusable design patterns.
Modules help you organize and reuse Terraform code across projects.
modules/vpc/ main.tf variables.tf outputs.tf README.md

# modules/vpc/main.tf resource "google_compute_network" "vpc" { name = var.name auto_create_subnetworks = false } resource "google_compute_subnetwork" "subnet" { for_each = var.subnets name = each.key ip_cidr_range = each.value.cidr region = each.value.region network = google_compute_network.vpc.id }
module "vpc" { source = "../modules/vpc" name = "prod-vpc" subnets = { "subnet-a" = { cidr = "10.0.1.0/24", region = "us-central1" } "subnet-b" = { cidr = "10.0.2.0/24", region = "us-east1" } } }
Use git tags or Terraform Registry for versioning. module "vpc" { source = "github.com/souravlobra/tf-modules//vpc?ref=v1.0.0" }
Keep modules focused on one thing Use semantic versioning Document inputs and outputs in README
